nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical marine and estuarine environments; commonly found in mangrove lagoons, protected bays, coastal canals, and shallow reef flats

summary: Fern Finger is a lightly calcified green macroalgae (Dasycladaceae) with upright, whorled-branch fronds resembling miniature ferns. Generally suited to display tanks in reef systems with stable parameters.
careNotes:
Fern Finger (Batophora sp.) is a lightly calcified green macroalgae in the family Dasycladaceae (Order Dasycladales), closely related to Neomeris. It produces erect thalli with whorled lateral branches giving a fern- or pipe-cleaner-like appearance. In the wild it tends to inhabit shallow tropical waters of the Caribbean and Western Atlantic (including Florida, the Gulf of Mexico, Belize, Costa Rica, Panama, Venezuela, and Bermuda), typically in mangrove lagoons, protected bays, and shallow coastal flats.

As a calcareous green alga, Batophora is generally considered more demanding than non-calcified macroalgae. Stable alkalinity (kH 8–12) and calcium levels (400–450 ppm) tend to support better growth; fluctuations in these parameters have been observed to cause tissue bleaching or melting. Medium lighting is generally recommended; excessive light may bleach the thallus, while insufficient light tends to result in slow or arrested growth. Growth rate is typically described as medium — slower than filamentous greens but faster than the most challenging calcareous forms such as Acetabularia or Penicillus.

This entry consolidates three Mosaic Macros SKUs: Fern Finger, Pipe-Cleaner Fern, and Hornwort Fern — all listed as Batophora sp. The Pipe-Cleaner Fern variant is described as 'fuzzier and more flexible than other Batophora species.' Mosaic Macros is currently the sole known aquarium hobby source for this genus; no AlgaeBarn or Top Shelf Aquatics listings were found. Care guidance has been extrapolated from family-level (Dasycladaceae) husbandry knowledge and general calcareous green macroalgae literature.

The most commonly referenced species in this genus for aquariums is B. oerstedii (also known as Fuzzy Finger Algae in invasion biology literature). A second species, B. occidentalis, is recognized but less documented in hobby contexts. The genus was established by J. Agardh in 1854.

Generally considered reef-safe; has been noted to be compatible with snails, hermit crabs, and most shrimp. Grazing fish and urchins may consume the thallus. Sold as 1–1.5 inch cuttings on plug or rock.
